name: Cetirizine
intakeConditions: with or without food
notes: Second-generation antihistamine. Non-sedating for most people; drowsiness possible. 24-hour allergy relief.

name: Loratadine
intakeConditions: with or without food
notes: Least sedating OTC antihistamine. 24-hour relief for allergic rhinitis and urticaria.

name: Fexofenadine
intakeConditions: with water; avoid fruit juice which significantly reduces absorption
notes: Non-sedating second-generation antihistamine. Fruit juice (grapefruit, orange, apple) markedly reduces bioavailability — use water only.

name: Diphenhydramine
intakeConditions: with or without food
notes: Sedating first-generation antihistamine. Strong anticholinergic effects. Not recommended for regular long-term use or in adults over 65.

name: Chlorpheniramine
intakeConditions: with food to reduce GI upset
notes: First-generation antihistamine. Sedating. Used for allergy symptoms, hives, and as part of combination cold medications.

name: Levocetirizine
intakeConditions: with or without food
notes: Active R-enantiomer of cetirizine with slightly greater potency. May cause drowsiness; preferred for evening dosing.

name: Omeprazole
intakeConditions: 30-60 minutes before first meal of the day; swallow whole
notes: Proton pump inhibitor (PPI). Used for GERD, ulcers, and Barrett's esophagus. Avoid long-term use without monitoring; depletes B12, magnesium, and other nutrients.

name: Esomeprazole
intakeConditions: 30-60 minutes before first meal
notes: PPI — S-enantiomer of omeprazole. Used for GERD, erosive esophagitis, and H. pylori eradication. Same nutrient depletion concerns as other PPIs.

name: Pantoprazole
intakeConditions: 30 minutes before a meal
notes: PPI used for GERD and Zollinger-Ellison syndrome. Often preferred for patients on multiple medications due to fewer CYP2C19 interactions.

name: Famotidine
intakeConditions: with or without food
notes: H2 receptor blocker. Used for heartburn, acid reflux, and peptic ulcers. Fewer long-term risks than PPIs. Can be used as needed.

name: Calcium Carbonate
intakeConditions: chew thoroughly; take after meals or as symptoms occur
notes: Fast-acting antacid for heartburn relief. Also provides supplemental calcium. Absorption requires adequate stomach acid — not ideal for those on PPIs.

name: Simethicone
intakeConditions: after meals and at bedtime
notes: Anti-foaming agent that relieves gas and bloating. Not absorbed systemically. Safe for all ages and during pregnancy.

name: Bismuth Subsalicylate
intakeConditions: with or without food
notes: Used for nausea, diarrhea, indigestion, and H. pylori eradication. Avoid in aspirin sensitivity and in children with viral illness (Reye's syndrome risk).

name: Loperamide
dose: 2-4mg initial, then 2mg after each loose stool
intakeConditions: with water
notes: Opioid receptor agonist that slows gut motility. Used for acute and traveler's diarrhea. Do not use with bloody diarrhea or fever.

name: Polyethylene Glycol 3350
intakeConditions: dissolve completely in 4-8 oz of liquid
notes: Osmotic laxative for constipation. Non-stimulant and non-habit forming. Generally produces a bowel movement within 1-3 days. Safe for short-term and longer use.

name: Docusate Sodium
intakeConditions: with a full glass of water
notes: Stool softener that draws water into the stool. Does not stimulate bowel movement. Used for constipation prevention and post-surgical or postpartum comfort.

name: Acetaminophen
intakeConditions: with or without food; do not exceed 3000-4000mg per day
notes: Analgesic and antipyretic. Does not affect platelets or the GI tract. Hepatotoxic in overdose or with heavy alcohol use — monitor total daily dose from all sources.

name: Ibuprofen
intakeConditions: with food or milk to reduce GI irritation
notes: NSAID with analgesic, anti-inflammatory, and antipyretic properties. Can cause GI bleeding, kidney stress, and increase cardiovascular risk with long-term use.

name: Naproxen Sodium
intakeConditions: with food or milk
notes: NSAID with longer duration of action (8-12 hours) than ibuprofen. Suitable for pain, inflammation, menstrual cramps, and fever.

name: Aspirin
intakeConditions: with food to reduce GI irritation; enteric-coated form available
notes: NSAID and antiplatelet agent. Low dose (81mg) used for cardiovascular prevention. Standard dose for pain, fever, and inflammation. Avoid in children with viral illness.

name: Acetaminophen + Caffeine
intakeConditions: with water; limit to 2 tablets in 24 hours
notes: Combination analgesic with caffeine as an adjunct for enhanced pain relief. Commonly used for migraine and tension headache.

name: Pseudoephedrine
intakeConditions: with water; available behind pharmacy counter
notes: Oral sympathomimetic decongestant. Effective for nasal congestion. Avoid in hypertension, hyperthyroidism, or with MAOIs. May cause insomnia if taken late in day.

name: Phenylephrine
intakeConditions: with or without food
notes: OTC oral decongestant. Evidence for efficacy is mixed at FDA-approved oral doses. Topical (nasal spray) formulation is more effective.

name: Oxymetazoline Nasal Spray
intakeConditions: do not use more than 3 consecutive days to avoid rebound congestion (rhinitis medicamentosa)
notes: Topical alpha-2 agonist nasal decongestant. Fast onset (within minutes). Highly effective for acute congestion. Strict 3-day limit to prevent rebound.

name: Guaifenesin
intakeConditions: with a full glass of water; increase fluid intake
notes: Expectorant that thins and loosens mucus. Used for chest congestion and cough. Increased water intake enhances effectiveness.

name: Doxylamine
intakeConditions: 30 minutes before bedtime
notes: First-generation antihistamine used as a sleep aid. Also used in pregnancy for nausea (combined with pyridoxine). Avoid with alcohol or other sedatives.

name: Diphenhydramine Sleep Aid
intakeConditions: 30 minutes before bedtime
notes: Antihistamine used as an OTC sleep aid. Tolerance develops rapidly with repeated use. Not recommended for regular use, in elderly patients, or long-term.

name: Hydrocortisone 1% Cream
intakeConditions: apply to affected area; avoid face, groin, and underarms with prolonged use
notes: Low-potency topical corticosteroid for eczema, insect bites, allergic contact dermatitis, and minor skin irritation. Avoid use beyond 7 days without guidance.

name: Clotrimazole
intakeConditions: clean and dry affected area before applying
notes: Topical antifungal for athlete's foot, ringworm, jock itch, and vaginal yeast infections (vaginal insert formulations available). Use full course even if symptoms resolve.

name: Miconazole
intakeConditions: insert at bedtime; topical apply twice daily
notes: Topical antifungal for vaginal yeast infections and skin fungal infections. Vaginal formulations available as 1-, 3-, and 7-day courses.

name: Bacitracin
intakeConditions: clean wound first; cover with bandage
notes: Topical antibiotic ointment for minor cuts, scrapes, and burns to prevent infection. Single-agent bacitracin preferred by some dermatologists over triple-antibiotic to reduce neomycin sensitization.

name: Benzoyl Peroxide
intakeConditions: apply to clean, dry skin; may bleach fabrics
notes: Topical antibacterial for acne. Kills P. acnes bacteria. Available in wash, gel, and cream forms. Can cause dryness and peeling — start with lower concentrations.

name: Salicylic Acid Topical
intakeConditions: apply to clean skin; avoid eye contact
notes: Beta-hydroxy acid (BHA) that exfoliates inside pores. Used for acne, psoriasis, calluses, and warts. Keratolytic — do not apply to large areas.

name: Artificial Tears
intakeConditions: tilt head back; do not touch dropper tip to eye
notes: Lubricating ophthalmic solution for dry eye relief. Preservative-free formulations preferred for frequent use (more than 4x/day) or sensitive eyes.

name: Ketotifen Eye Drops
intakeConditions: do not wear contact lenses within 10 minutes of application
notes: OTC antihistamine and mast cell stabilizer eye drops for allergic conjunctivitis. Duration of action 8-12 hours. Safe for extended use.

name: Olopatadine Eye Drops
intakeConditions: do not wear contact lenses within 10 minutes of application
notes: OTC antihistamine and mast cell stabilizer eye drops. Once-daily formulation provides 24-hour relief for allergic eye symptoms.

name: Zinc Acetate Lozenges
intakeConditions: start within 24 hours of cold symptom onset; dissolve slowly in mouth; do not chew
notes: Releases ionic zinc to inhibit viral replication in nasal passages. Evidence supports reducing cold duration when started early. May cause nausea if taken on empty stomach.

name: Metformin
intakeConditions: with meals to reduce GI side effects; extended-release taken with evening meal
notes: First-line oral medication for type 2 diabetes. Also used off-label for PCOS, insulin resistance, and longevity. Depletes B12 — monitor levels with long-term use.

name: Levothyroxine
intakeConditions: on empty stomach, 30-60 minutes before food, coffee, or other medications
notes: Synthetic T4 thyroid hormone replacement. Absorption impaired by food, coffee, calcium, iron, and certain medications. Consistent morning timing is essential.

name: Liothyronine
intakeConditions: on empty stomach
notes: Synthetic T3 thyroid hormone. Active form with faster onset than T4. Used when T4-to-T3 conversion is impaired. Shorter half-life requires twice-daily dosing.

name: Low Dose Naltrexone
intakeConditions: at bedtime; compounded formulation required
notes: Off-label immune-modulating use of low-dose naltrexone. Used for autoimmune conditions, fibromyalgia, chronic pain, and inflammatory bowel disease. Bedtime dosing coincides with endorphin surge.

name: Progesterone
intakeConditions: take at bedtime with food (peanut oil-based capsule)
notes: Bioidentical micronized progesterone. Used for HRT, luteal phase support, and sleep. Sedating at higher doses — bedtime preferred. Peanut allergy caution for Prometrium.

name: Estradiol Patch
intakeConditions: apply to clean, dry skin on lower abdomen or buttocks; rotate sites; avoid waistband area
notes: Transdermal bioidentical estradiol for menopausal HRT. Transdermal route avoids first-pass hepatic metabolism and carries lower clot risk than oral estrogens.

name: Estradiol Cream
intakeConditions: apply with applicator vaginally or to vulvar tissue as directed
notes: Local estradiol for genitourinary syndrome of menopause (vaginal dryness, dyspareunia). Minimal systemic absorption. Can also be applied topically to labia or vulvar areas.

name: Testosterone Cypionate
intakeConditions: intramuscular or subcutaneous injection; rotate sites
notes: Long-acting injectable testosterone ester. Used for hypogonadism and TRT. Monitor hematocrit, PSA, estradiol, and testosterone levels. Subcutaneous administration increasingly preferred.

name: Testosterone Cream
intakeConditions: apply to inner wrist, inner thigh, or labia; rotate sites; avoid skin-to-skin contact after application
notes: Transdermal testosterone for hypogonadism in men and women. Women use much lower doses (1-5mg). Transfer to partners and children must be avoided.

name: Desiccated Thyroid
intakeConditions: on empty stomach, 30-60 minutes before food and other medications
notes: Natural thyroid extract containing both T4 and T3. Preferred by some patients over levothyroxine alone. Dosing in grains (60mg = 1 grain). Monitor TSH, free T4, free T3.

name: Spironolactone
intakeConditions: with food to reduce GI upset; monitor potassium levels
notes: Potassium-sparing diuretic and antiandrogen. Used for hormonal acne, androgenic hair loss, PCOS, and hyperaldosteronism. Monitor potassium, especially with ACE inhibitors.

name: Finasteride
intakeConditions: with or without food
notes: 5-alpha reductase inhibitor that reduces DHT. Used for androgenic alopecia and BPH. Potential sexual side effects. Post-finasteride syndrome reported in a subset of users.

name: Minoxidil
intakeConditions: topical: apply to dry scalp twice daily; oral: with or without food
notes: Vasodilator that prolongs hair follicle anagen phase. Topical for androgenic alopecia. Low-dose oral increasingly used off-label. Results require sustained use.

name: Clomiphene
intakeConditions: taken on specific days of menstrual cycle as directed
notes: Selective estrogen receptor modulator (SERM). Used for ovulation induction in anovulatory infertility and off-label for male hypogonadism to stimulate LH/FSH.

name: Letrozole
intakeConditions: with or without food; taken on specific days of cycle
notes: Aromatase inhibitor. Off-label first-line agent for ovulation induction (preferred over clomiphene for PCOS). Also used in male hypogonadism and estrogen management in TRT.

name: Ivermectin
intakeConditions: on empty stomach with water
notes: Antiparasitic agent used for onchocerciasis, strongyloidiasis, scabies, head lice, and rosacea. Topical formulation available for rosacea. Weight-based dosing.

name: Hydroxychloroquine
intakeConditions: with food or milk to reduce GI upset
notes: Antimalarial with immune-modulating properties. Used for lupus, rheumatoid arthritis, and Sjogren's syndrome. Annual retinal eye exam required with long-term use.

name: Prednisone
intakeConditions: with food to reduce GI irritation; take in morning to minimize adrenal suppression
notes: Systemic corticosteroid. Broad anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressive. Short-term use for acute inflammation; long-term use requires tapering and monitoring for adrenal suppression.

name: Modafinil
intakeConditions: with or without food; take early in the day to avoid insomnia
notes: Wakefulness-promoting agent used for narcolepsy, shift work disorder, and sleep apnea. Used off-label for cognitive enhancement and fatigue. Schedule IV controlled substance.

name: Methotrexate
intakeConditions: weekly dosing only — daily dosing is dangerous
notes: Disease-modifying antirheumatic drug (DMARD). Weekly oral or injectable use for rheumatoid arthritis, psoriasis, and inflammatory conditions. Requires folate supplementation and monitoring of CBC and LFTs.

name: Doxylamine + Pyridoxine
intakeConditions: take at bedtime; extended-release formulation preferred
notes: First-line pharmacological treatment for nausea and vomiting of pregnancy (morning sickness). FDA category A in pregnancy. Begin with bedtime dose; titrate based on symptoms.
